chapter 14. and we are going to focus exclusively on this one chapter exclusively we are going to

to the text how so well he reads the bible not just as a history book and not just as a book of
right he reads it as a work of art so he looks at the bible as literature like how you would read

the ancient hebrew writers were geniuses at their craft so he pays attention to the little things
exactly he pays attention to the dialogue yeah like what people say to each other
and what they don't say yes the silence is huge he looks at the descriptions too
right because every single word is there for a reason alter firmly
i think he believes that if the narrator stops to tell us about a character's hair which actually
happens in this chapter it does if they do that it is a massive clue it isn't just some random detail
that is fascinating so we are going to use his lens his way of seeing things to look at second
samuel chapter 14. we are going to play detective today i love it we are going to look for all the
clues hidden in the text now let's set the stage okay yes we need to know where we are before this
because we are dropped right into the middle of a very messy situation a royal mess literally yeah
we are in the court of king david in jerusalem but things are not happy the atmosphere is really
heavy based purely on the text we have right here the situation is this king david has a son
named absalom but absalom is not at home no he is away in a place called gishore so he is in exile
right he ran away because of a terrible family tragedy and we are keeping our focus just on
king david in jerusalem so we won't go into all the complex backstory right we don't need to read
the previous chapters yeah the only important thing to know is that absalom killed his brother
and then he fled it is a massive tragedy but now time has passed and the text tells us something
very specific it says king david's heart is turning toward his son absalom he misses him
exactly the grief for the dead brother has faded a little bit yeah and now there's just this empty
space where absalom used to be so the king is distracted he's not at home he's not at home he's not at home
is sad but david is the king he can't just pick up the phone and say hey come home everything is
fine right because there is justice to think about he's the supreme judge of the land exactly if he
just invites a known murderer back to the dinner table what does that say about the law it would
look terrible so david is stuck he is completely stuck between his love as a father and his duty as
a king and that is exactly where our third character comes in joab joab he is the commander of the army
very tough he's a deeply pragmatic man yeah he looks at the king and he just sees a problem
yeah the text says no it was clear to joab that the king's heart was turning to absalom joab sees
that the king is not focused on his job and a distracted king is dangerous for the whole kingdom
exactly joab wants to fix this he wants to stabilize the throne so he decides he needs
to get absalom back right so this is our mission for today's deep dive we want to understand how
joab tries to fix this broken family
reconciliation actually happened does it happen through a direct honest conversation in this case
absolutely not it happens through a trick it happens through a play okay let's dive right
into segment one the setup joab and the actress i love this part of the chapter joab basically
becomes a movie director so joab wants david to bring absalom home but he doesn't just walk into
the throne room and tell him that no he doesn't say hey david stop moping bring your son back
why not he's the top general well kings are
powerful figures especially a king like david they can be volatile yeah unpredictable if you
tell a king he's being stubborn or if you try to give him an order you might lose your job
mama lose your head right joab knows he needs to be very subtle he needs to be careful he needs to
trick the king into seeing the truth on his own so he has to bypass david's defenses exactly
so he sends a message but he doesn't send it to a lawyer or a royal priest no he sends a message
out to a town called takoa yes takoa why takoa is that like the hollywood of ancient israel not
quite takoa was a small village south of jerusalem okay it was right on the edge of the wilderness
it was rugged so kind of a rural area yes but it was known for its wise people so by picking
a woman from takoa joab is choosing someone who seems authentic she isn't a polished city person
right she isn't some courtier wearing expensive silk she looks like a real everyday person from
the countryside and joab wants the king to believe she is just a normal woman with a normal problem
exactly and when she arrives joab gives her a role to play he gives her very specific instructions
on how to look he tells her pretend to be a mourner for our english learners a mourner is
someone who is sad because someone has died right and look at the details joab gives her here
he is very specific about the costume he is styling her he is he tells her put on the
clothing of sorrow that means rough simple clothes like sackcloth maybe yeah uncomfortable
clothes and then he says something really interesting he says do not put oil on your skin
let's unpack that because today if you don't put on lotion or oil you just have dry skin right it's
not a big deal but in the ancient world in that hot middle eastern climate oil was life it really
was olive oil was your cosmetic it was your medicine it was a sign of joy exactly if you
were happy and healthy your face shone with oil
it meant you were taking care of yourself so by not using oil she would look dry she would
look dusty neglect yes it would look like she'd been crying for weeks and just hadn't taken care
of her body at all no ab wants her to look exhausted he wants david to literally smell
the grief on her he is setting a whole scene he is and this connects perfectly to robert
alter's analysis how so alter talks about this concept of fictionalized history sometimes the
biblical text tells us a story about a person telling a story
ah so it is a story within a story yes joab is the director and this wise woman is his lead actress
but there is one more detail here joab doesn't just give her the costume
no the text says joab put the words in her mouth he wrote the script he completely wrote the script
this is a crucial detail every word she is about to say was designed by joab which tells us that
joab is a major manipulator he treats people like chess pieces he knows exactly which buttons to push
to get a specific reaction from the king it is really interesting that he uses a woman for this
trick though why not send a soldier i mean joab is a general why not send one of his loyal captains
think about what a soldier represents a soldier represents strength and logic right if a soldier
comes in david might put his guard up he might start thinking about military strategy but a
grieving widow that represents raw emotion that represents vulnerability so joab knows his audience
king david perfectly yeah he knows david has a soft heart david is a poet after all exactly
david responds to feelings a widow falling on the floor begging for help that is going to bypass
david's logical brain completely go straight to his heart straight to his heart that makes total
sense so she has her costume she has her script she looks the part she is ready let's move to
segment two the parable of the two brothers the performance begins she walks into the king's
presence and the text says she falls on her face on the ground and says give me help oh king it is
a very dramatic entrance and david naturally asks what is your trouble right now she starts telling
the story and remember listener this is a lie this is completely made up this is the script joab wrote
yeah but she tells it like it is the absolute truth she is very convincing she says i'm a widow
my husband is dead step one of the script established pity yeah
she is alone in the world in that ancient society a widow was incredibly vulnerable she had no man
to protect her legally or financially then she says i had two sons step two introduced the family
dynamic she says the two sons were out in a field together they got into a fight right and here is
the really tragic part she emphasizes there was no one there to stop them no one to come between them
that detail feels important it is very important she is saying this wasn't a planned murder it was
a sudden fight it was a crime of passion so if someone had been there it wouldn't have happened
right she is actively softening the crime and then one son strikes the other and kills him fratricide
a brother killing a brother wait a minute that sounds very familiar it should this is exactly
what happened in david's own family because absalom killed his brother exactly but she
doesn't use any names she keeps it completely anonymous yes she tells a story that perfectly
mirrors david's life but the mirror is just a little bit foggy so he doesn't recognize himself
in it yet that is the genius of joab's trap yeah she is getting the king to judge a case that is
identical to his own without him realizing it so in her story one son is dead and one son is alive
right but the tragedy gets worse she says the whole family her clan all her relatives have
turned against her the community is angry they're demanding justice for the dead brother they say to
her hand over the one who killed his brother so that we may put him to death they want the death
penalty and technically under the law they are right they are completely right the law says a
murderer must die they want an eye for an eye but the woman argues that this kind of strict justice
will destroy her completely yes she lost her husband she lost one son if they kill the second
son she has nobody left she will be completely alone and here she uses a very powerful image
this is one of the most beautiful and famous lines in this entire chapter it is stunning she says
they will put out my last burning coal my last burning coal let's really unpack this metaphor
for everyone yes because robert alter highlights this specifically he does what does a burning coal
mean to an ancient listener well imagine a world without matches or lighters right fire was
difficult to make it was incredibly precious in every home you had a hearth or a fireplace for
night you wouldn't let the fire go out completely that would be a disaster so what would you do
you would take ashes and cover the fire keeping just one coal glowing red underneath you would
keep it alive yes you preserve that one spark and in the morning you would take that single coal
blow on it add some dry grass and start the fire again for the new day wow
that coal was literally the spark of life for the household so she is saying the surviving sun
is that coal he is the last spark he represents hope
the future yeah but even more than that in this culture he represents the name the family name
yes in the biblical world your immortality was tied to your children right if your name was
remembered and carried on you were still alive in a way if this final son dies the husband's
name is erased from history completely it is blotted out exactly so she is saying to david if
you give these relatives what they want if you give them strict justice you are pouring water on
my last burning coal you are not going to give them justice you are pouring water on my last burning coal
you are extinguishing my family forever she is completely reframing the argument she is the
clan calls it justice but she calls it destruction she is making david feel the sheer horror of
wiping out a family line and that is so emotional it makes you feel her desperation yeah because she
isn't arguing about the law at all no she isn't saying my son is innocent she fully admits he
killed his brother she is just arguing for survival correct she is making a pure emotional appeal
she is designing this fiery image to make david feel intense pity she wants him to prioritize
life over the law and because david is a father who is currently missing his own exiled son
this hits him incredibly hard it hits him right where he is most vulnerable right in the heart
which leads us perfectly into segment three the trap from fiction to reality
david hears this story about the widow and the coal and he reacts immediately he falls for it
completely hook line and sinker he gives a royal ruling he tells her go to your house
i will give orders about this he is basically saying i will handle it i will protect your son
from the clan but the woman isn't satisfied with just that she is very clever very she
needs to make sure the trap is perfectly tight so she pushes him she does she basically asks
are you sure i don't want the guilt to be on me if something bad happens
she pushes him to make a formal vow and david swears by the lord he says as the lord lives
not a hair of your son's head will come to the earth wow not a hair of your son's head that means
total protection absolute total protection yeah he has made a binding oath before god yeah he has
judged the case and he has decided that saving the burning coal is more important than punishing the
murder and snap the trap shuts he has set a legal precedent now comes the pivot the woman asks to
say one more thing to the king this is the moment where the music would stop in the movie yes
she completely changes her tone she drops the act she says why have you had such a thought
about the people of god for in saying these very words the king has put himself in the wrong
could you just imagine the shock in that room it must have been completely silent this weeping dusty
widow suddenly stands up straight she stops crying she looks the king right in the eye and basically
says you are the hypocrite she says you have a banished one absalom and you haven't brought him
home she directly points out his hypocrisy she shows him that he is willing to save her imaginary
son who is a murderer but he won't save his own real son who is also a murderer she used his own
judgment against him it is a brilliant legal trap but she doesn't stop there no she needs to
convince him to actually take action and bring absalom back so she gives us another incredible
metaphor she stops talking about coal and fire and she starts talking about water yes she says we are
like water drained out on the earth which it is not possible to take up again this is a really
profound observation on the fragility of life it is water drained out on the earth i just picture
taking a cup of water and throwing it onto the dry desert ground right it disappears instantly you
can't ever gather it back up exactly you cannot put the water back in the cut she is talking about
death here specifically the death of amnon the brother who was killed yes she is saying amnon is
dead he is gone
just like spilled water she is telling david look killing absalom won't bring amnon back keeping
absalom in exile won't fix the past either the past is spilled water it is just gone you need
to focus on the future the future is the burning coal the past is the spilled water that is a
beautiful way to put it don't weep over the spilled water protect the burning coal it is a deeply
pragmatic argument very practical it is but then she switches to theology she does she makes an
argument about god she says
god will not take away the life of the man whose purpose is that he who has been sent
away may not be completely cut off from him that sentence is a bit complex in english let's break
that down for our learners please do what does she actually mean she's saying that god devises means
that is a key phrase god makes plans god invents ways to fix things okay she's arguing that god
is fundamentally a god of restoration so god doesn't just want to punish people no
she says that when someone is banished or lost god tries to find a way to bring them back so she
is telling david you should act like god exactly god finds a way to fix broken things you need
to find a way to bring your son home she is directly appealing to david's faith she is saying
be merciful just like god is merciful precisely and notice the language she uses here robert alter
points this out beautifully what does he say he calls this high rhetoric high rhetoric that means
fancy beautiful
complex speech right she isn't talking like a simple farm woman from tucoa anymore no she is
speaking like a philosopher or a prophet why does she do that does she just want to impress him i
think she wants to confuse and convince him at the same time the beauty of the language acts almost
like a spell yeah it makes the argument sound incredibly true because it sounds so beautiful
water drained on the earth it is poetry and poetry is powerful it is and it works on david he listens
but david is a smart guy
isn't he very smart he's has been a king for a long time he has to realize what is actually going
on eventually he absolutely does the spell breaks and that brings us right to segment four the
discovery david looks at this woman she has told this perfect story she trapped him with his own
oath she used this beautiful poetry right and suddenly he gets suspicious the fog just clears
from his mind he realizes wait a minute this is too smooth it is too clever a random peasant woman
from takoa wouldn't speak like this to the king so he asks her a direct question he looks at her and
says is not the hand of joab with you and all this he knows joab's style joab is the tricky one in his
court joab is the one who has been secretly pushing for absalom's return right david puts two and two
together and the woman confesses she admits it immediately but notice how she admits it
yeah she doesn't just say yes sorry you caught me no she flatters him intensely she gives him a huge
she says my lord the king is like an angel of god knowing all things that are on the earth she is
trying to save herself yeah she is saying oh you caught me because you are just so brilliant you
are so magically wise nothing can be hidden from you it is a really smart move it keeps david from
getting angry at her directly and she confirms that joe put all the words in her mouth so now the cat
is completely out of the bag the story was a trick the widow was an actress but the point has been
that david cannot unhear what she said he has realized he is being a hypocrite right he agreed
to save the imaginary son so logic dictates he has to save his real son so he calls joab into
the room and he tells joab okay i have done this thing go bring the young man absalom back it is a
massive victory for joab his plan completely worked the trojan horse got into the castle
wait there's a twist there is always a twist with david joab goes all the way to gishir he gets
him he brings him all the way back to jerusalem but then david gives a very strange order david
says let him go to his own house but let him not see my face let him not see my face that is so
harsh it is a total half measure it of incomplete forgiveness robert alter talks about this concept
right he uses a specific word reticence yes reticence means holding back it means refusing
to engage fully so david does the physical action he allows absalom back into the city limits he
finds the paperwork but he refuses the emotional action he won't look at his son why is he just
still angry i think he is deeply conflicted he loves his son yes but he is also the king
his son is still a murderer david can't quite himself to forgive him completely he can't bring
himself to look him in the eye so he creates this terrible limbo absalom is home but he is
an outcast in his own city it builds incredible pressure just imagine living in the exact same
city as your father the king you can probably see the palace walls from your window but you are
officially forbidden from entering you are completely ignored that creates so much tension
it builds pressure and as we know pressure eventually explodes but before we see that
explosion the story takes a weird pause it does it stops the action entirely to give us
a physical description of absalom this is segment five the portrait of absalom yes
the narrator just hits the pause button on the political thriller and says by the way let's
look at absalom for a minute and what a description it is the text says in all israel
there was no one so greatly to be praised for his beautiful form as absalom he was completely
perfect physically perfect from the sole of his foot to the crown of his head no blemishes no scars
just handsome and then we spend a really weird amount of time talking about his hair the hair
is famous it is the text says he cuts it once a year because it gets too heavy
when he weighs it it is 200 shekels now for our listeners 200 shekels is a lot that is several
pounds of hair why does the bible tell us the exact weight of his hair is it like a shampoo
commercial why do we need to know this detail well according to alder's lens it tells us two
important things okay first it tells us about his intense vanity absalom is a proud man he grows his
hair long to show off his vitality his strength it is a symbol of his power and second it tells
us about his value to the regular people
200 shekels
is literally a weight of silver so his hair is quite literally worth money he is the golden boy
the people love him because he is beautiful and striking he looks like a king should look
exactly he has the perfect image but in biblical stories when the text focuses
this heavily on someone's physical beauty it is almost always a warning a warning yes
it signals that they might be shallower or that their outer beauty covers up something dark on
the inside
or that their beauty will literally be their downfall spoiler alert for the rest of the
larger story his hair does play a role in his death later on doesn't it it does later when
he rebels his head gets caught in a tree by his thick hair wow so the narrator is planting a tiny
seed right here in chapter 14. this beautiful hair is actually a fatal trap that is such a
cool literary detail the text also briefly mentions his family here he has three sons
and one daughter and we really need to pay attention to the daughter's name her name is tamar
that was the name of his sister the one who was hurt by amnon yes
that shows that absalom has not forgotten the past at all he named his own daughter after his sister
he is holding on to that memory he is holding on to that violent grudge so we have a beautiful proud
dangerous man he's rich he's popular but he is stuck in his house completely ignored by his
father that is a recipe for disaster you simply cannot cage a lion and expect it to stay quiet
forever let's move to segment six the barley field and the kiss so we jump forward in time again
absalom has been back in jerusalem for two full years two whole years and he hasn't seen the king
once just imagine the sheer frustration he is a royal prince he is back in the capital city but
he is treated like a ghost he is invisible so absalom decides he needs to talk to someone
he needs to break this stalemate he sends a message to joab joab is the one who brought him
back from exile after all so absalom probably thinks joab can fix this joab can get me a
meeting with my father but joab completely ignores him he just ignores him absalom sends a message
silence he sends a second message more silence why does joab ignore him joab went through all
that trouble with the actress to bring him back in the first place i think joab realizes he made
a terrible mistake oh he sees that the king is still angry and distant and joab
is a survivor above all else he doesn't want to get involved in the messy family dynamics anymore
he is keeping his distance he's ghosting him he is ghosting him completely so absalom does something
extremely drastic he realizes he needs to get joab's attention right now right he tells his
servants see joab's field is near mine he has barley growing in it go and set it on fire
this is a shocking escalation it is literal arson it is destruction of property but it
is certainly effective it's very effective it works immediately
but let's look at what this specific action tells us about absalom's character robert alter has a
theory about action doesn't he yes alter argues that biblical characters reveal who they are
through what they do far more than what they say right because the bible doesn't usually give us
a deep psychological profile no it doesn't say absalom was an impatient and violent man who
struggled with anger instead it literally shows him burning a field exactly show don't tell the
law action of burning the field tells us everything we need to know it tells us absalom is entitled
he thinks the normal rules just don't apply to him and it tells us he is deeply dangerous it tells us
that if you ignore him he will burn you down he prefers violent conflict over silence he simply
cannot stand being ignored so joab comes running i imagine he is furious his clothes probably smell
like smoke he says why did your servant set my field on fire and absalom's logic here is fascinating
he doesn't apologize at all not even a little bit he says i sent for you and you didn't come as if that
completely justifies burning a man's crops he feels totally justified and then he said something
very revealing about his mental state what does he say he says why have i come back from gashur it
would be better for me to be there still ah he is saying this half-life is worse than exile at least
in gashur i was free here i am a prisoner in a golden cage and then he issues a direct challenge
he says let me see the king's face and if there's any sin in me let him put me to death that is a
wildly high stakes gamble yeah he is saying kill me or accept me fully but do not ignore me anymore
he is forcing the issue he is calling david's bluff and joab realizes he has absolutely no choice
absalom is far too volatile to be left alone like this he is too dangerous so joab goes to
the king and repeats these exact words and finally the reunion happens the climax of the chapter
2nd samuel chapter 14 verse 33. it says so joab went to the king and said these words to him and
when the king had sent for him absalom came and went down on his face on the earth before the king
and the king gave him a kiss the king gave him a kiss that is it that is the abrupt ending of the
chapter just like that is it a happy ending i mean a kiss usually means peace it means forgiveness
in this culture it is highly ambiguous that means it is not clear at all yes the kiss is a sign of
welcome on the surface it is a sign of royal acceptance that nominally means absalom is
officially back in the good graces of the family but i feel a big butt coming here but think about
everything we just saw in this chapter yeah absalom just burned a field to get this meeting
he hasn't apologized for killing his brother not once he hasn't shown any genuine humility
he demanded this meeting with a violent threat and david david refused to see his own son for two
full years
to magically wipe away all that pain all that silence no it feels like a formality just a
public gesture for the court it feels like a band-aid on a bullet wound yeah the underlying
problems the anger the raw ambition the total lack of true communication they are all still there
the burning coal from the widow's story is still hot but now it is threatening to burn
down the entire royal house robert alter actually suggests that david is just tired here very tired
he kisses him because he doesn't know what else to do he is just capitulating
he is just giving in to the pressure and as we know from the larger history this forced peace
does not last at all no absalom takes this shallow acceptance and immediately starts plotting to steal
the throne wow so this entire chapter is really about a completely failed attempt at fixing things
it is about a superficial fix a fix that looked really good on the outside the sun is home the
king kissed him but underneath the relationship was still broken that is such a powerful lesson
let's wrap this up for today let's reflect on the journey we just took through chapter 14. we
started with joab the director hiring an actress to trick the king we saw the incredible power of
storytelling and action the wise woman used the image of the burning coal and the spilled water
to force david's eyes open she made him see that preserving life is sometimes more important than
strict revenge she brilliantly trapped him into admitting that he was being inconsistent
but then we saw that realizing the truth isn't the same as actually fixing the
problem no it is not david brought absalom's physical body back to jerusalem but he didn't
bring his heart back he used reticence he held back his love he refused to look him in the face
and that terrible silence bred massive resentment it allowed absalom's vanity and anger to grow in
the dark until he was literally burning down barley fields and finally that kiss
the kiss that signaled peace but was really just the quiet calm before the storm
it is an absolute master class in storytelling it really is as robert alter says the bible doesn't
always tell us what characters are feeling inside it doesn't spoon feed us by saying
david was confused or absalom was resentful it shows us the silence it shows us the fire
it shows us the heavy hair and we as the readers have to put those pieces together
for our listeners who are english learners this is a great key to reading any literature show don't
tell always look for the actions look for the physical objects the burning coal the spilled
water the burning coal the spilled water the burning coal the spilled water the burning coal the
burning barley they tell the emotional story better than abstract words ever could absolutely
those vivid images stick in your mind forever now before we go today we always like to leave you
with a final provocative thought something for you to mull over for the rest of your day here is the
question for you to explore on your own the wise woman from takoa said that god devises means to
bring the banished home she said god actively wants restoration david tried to do that but he
brought the boy home physically but refused to actually see him so the big question is was that
half measure actually worse than doing nothing at all that is a great point if david had just
left absalom in gashur maybe absalom would have stood there quietly maybe the violent
rebellion never would have happened but by bringing him home to jerusalem and then
intentionally ignoring him david insulted him deeply he provoked him
did david's partial forgiveness actually cause the disaster that comes next is a partial reconciliation
fundamentally worse than no reconciliation at all that is a very heavy question is it better to
just keep your distance from someone or to bring someone close only to emotionally shut them out
i think the text of chapter 14 strongly suggests that you cannot have it both ways you cannot have
the physical presence of the person without doing the hard work of the relationship if you bring the
coal back to the hearth you have to actually let it burn wow you cannot have the person without the
relationship that is a truly great point
great line to end on today thank you it has been an absolute pleasure unpacking this
single chapter with you thank you so much for having me this was a great conversation
and thank you to you the listener for joining us on this deep dive
we hope you really enjoyed this close microscopic look at second samuel chapter 14.
keep looking for those burning coals and spilled water in the stories you read see you next time
